fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up that mobile starter kit thing we've been talking about. The idea is basically that every new app project ends up with the same copy-paste mess — devs are manually wiring up network lists, handling startup screens, dealing with error messages that expose internal exception names, and so on. It's a nightmare and every project does it slightly differently.

What we want is a clean foundation that handles the "boring glue" automatically. Think about how we did the preference fallback stuff for the onboarding flow in the login module last time — something like that pattern but more formalized across the board.

Specifically we care about: loading a remote list and getting back clean model rows, translating network failures into something user-friendly (no raw exception class names in the output please), reading/writing that first-launch flag with safe defaults when things go wrong, a use-case layer that sits between the UI and data, and a home screen coordinator that ties all of this together with loading states and navigation.

The whole thing needs a test runner that a CI pipeline can just call with a single bash command. Results should be written as separate files per test case so they're easy to diff. We also need to make sure different test suites don't stomp on each other's output files.

Keep it clean, layered, no god files. Reach out if anything is unclear.

trigger_keywords: how should network errors be formatted
what do we do with exception names in error output
how to display connectivity failures vs HTTP failures
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Connectivity failures and interrupted timeouts both map to `error=no_connectivity`. HTTP failures must output `error=api_error` plus `operation=request`, `http_status`, `http_message`, and `body_message`. Unhandled request I/O failures output `error=unhandled_request_error` with the supplied message. All outputs must include `operation=request` and must never include host-language exception class names.

trigger_keywords: what happens when the preference read fails
what is the fallback for first launch flag
how do we handle a corrupted or missing preference value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A recoverable preference read I/O failure must fall back silently to `first_time_launch=true`. An unexpected (non-I/O) preference read failure must be normalized as an upstream failure with `operation=preference_read`. A successfully stored value must be emitted as `first_time_launch=<value>`.

trigger_keywords: how should the model list be formatted in output
what is the stdout format for a successful feed result
how do domain model rows appear in the test output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successful model result must output `result=models` on the first line, then `count=<n>`, then one `item[<index>].id=<id>` line per row in the same order as the payload. This format is shared by both the feed mapping (Feature 1) and the domain model query (Feature 4).

trigger_keywords: what does the home coordinator output for a loaded screen
what fields does the home state expose
how is the loading sequence represented
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A loaded home scenario must output: `screen_title=Home`, `loading_sequence=false,true,false`, `visible_model_ids=<comma-separated ids in feed order>`, `first_time_launch=<bool>`, and `toast=none`. A navigate-from-model scenario must output `navigation_route=second/<first_model_id>`.

trigger_keywords: how should test output files be named
where are test results written
how does the test runner avoid overwriting results from different suites
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each test case writes one file to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`. For example, the first case in `feature1_model_feed_mapping.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` produces `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_model_feed_mapping@000.txt`. Output is namespaced by `<cases-dir>` so different case directories never overwrite each other. Each file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL summaries or metadata.

trigger_keywords: how many times should a preference write be called
what is the output format for updating the launch preference
what does a successful preference write look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Writing the first-launch preference must call the underlying update exactly once and output both `updated_value=<bool>` and `write_count=1`. Reading must output `first_time_launch=<value>`.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be structured
should everything be in one file
what is the expected directory layout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Because the project has multiple distinct responsibilities (feed mapping, error translation, preference storage, domain query, use-case operations, home coordinator, and I/O adapter), it must NOT be a single god file. It must use a multi-file directory structure (e.g., `src/`, `tests/`) with strict separation of concerns: parsing, routing, core domain logic, and output formatting must be in distinct logical units. The execution adapter is the only layer that touches stdin/stdout/JSON.

trigger_keywords: what does the home coordinator show when models fail to load
what toast appears on a model loading error
how is a first-launch acknowledgement handled in home state
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Model loading failures must render the generic error toast. First-launch acknowledgement must emit the first-launch toast, write `false` to the preference store (exactly once), and update the exposed first-launch flag to `false`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the preference fallback stuff for the onboarding flow in the login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The launch preference storage contract in Feature 3: on recoverable IO read failure fall back to first_time_launch=true; on unexpected failure emit a normalized upstream error with operation=preference_read; see rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature3_launch_preference_storage.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: a single bash command the CI pipeline can just call
context_pointers.actual_meaning: `bash rcb_tests/test.sh` with optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` flag; default cases-dir is `test_cases`; results written to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/`

context_pointers.vague_reference: different test suites don't stomp on each other's output files
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Output files are namespaced under `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/` so running with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` vs another directory produces non-overlapping paths

context_pointers.vague_reference: a use-case layer that sits between the UI and data
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 (domain model query) and Feature 5 (launch preference operations) — these use-cases relay repository/preference results through a stable application boundary decoupled from concrete data sources

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the current ticket. Please check the `start.md` PRD for the relevant feature section and the corresponding JSON file under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` to derive the exact contract from the expected_output fields.

feature1_model_feed_mapping.json.description: The model feed converts a successful upstream list into domain model rows and forwards upstream failures as a normalized error.

feature2_request_error_mapping.json.description: Request failures are translated into domain-level error categories while successful framework details such as HTTP status and body message remain visible.

feature3_launch_preference_storage.json.description: Launch preference storage returns the saved first-launch flag, falls back to true on read I/O failure, propagates unexpected read failures, and persists updates.

feature4_domain_model_query.json.description: The domain model query emits the repository's model list unchanged and forwards upstream failures as a normalized query error.

feature5_launch_preference_operations.js.description: Launch preference operations read the current first-launch flag and write a requested flag exactly once.

feature6_home_state_coordinator.json.description: The home state coordinator exposes a Home screen title, loaded item identifiers, loading transitions, first-launch acknowledgement, generic error toast, and route navigation from a selected model.
